expectations.text: Contains a concrete analogy using familiar real-world concepts
expectations.evidence: Coffee shop analogy — ordering a latte, barista promising to call your name, stepping aside to wait.

expectations.text: Explicitly states where the analogy breaks down
expectations.evidence: 'Where this analogy breaks down: In a real coffee shop, you physically wait in one place. In JavaScript, await doesn't block the whole program...'

expectations.text: Includes a visual (tldraw, Mermaid, or structured)
expectations.evidence: Mermaid flowchart with colored nodes showing order → promise → wait → success/error paths.

expectations.text: The visual teaches something the text alone does not
expectations.evidence: Visual shows the branching path (success vs. error) and the sequential flow with color coding — the error path fork is clearer visually than in text.
One-Liner → Analogy → Visual → line-by-line code walkthrough → 'Part that trips people up' → Go Deeper.

expectations.text: Includes curated external links with descriptions
expectations.evidence: Four links (javascript.info, freeCodeCamp, MDN, The Odin Project) each with one-sentence descriptions of value.

expectations.text: Technical terms introduced only after being tied to familiar concept
expectations.evidence: 'Promise' = barista's promise, 'await' = stepping aside, 'resolved' = name called. Each term mapped to analogy first.

expectations.text: No single section introduces more than 4 new concepts
expectations.evidence: Code walkthrough goes line by line, introducing one concept per block. Detail section focuses on async vs. sync misconception only.

expectations.text: References the specific code snippet provided
expectations.evidence: Complete line-by-line walkthrough of fetchUserData, referencing fetch(), response.json(), try/catch, and throw error from the original code.
